raw_indicator_text:
Beskrivelse af de vigtigste negative indvirkninger på bæredygtighedsfaktorer
Nedenfor følger et skema, som Jyske Bank-koncernen er forpligtiget til at offentliggøre. Det indeholder datapunkter fra ultimo 2023 og 2024 for en række PAI-
indikatorer samt en beskrivelse af vores foranstaltninger.
En af vores anvendte foranstaltninger er aktivt ejerskab, som betyder, at vi som investor og dermed ejer af selskaber arbejder aktivt med at påvirke selskaberne
i en mere bæredygtig retning. Aktivt ejerskab består typisk af dialog med selskaberne og/eller af stemmeafgivelse på generalforsamlinger.
En anden foranstaltning vi anvender er CO2 reduktionsmål. Et CO2 reduktionsmål betyder, at vi årligt arbejder for at reducere den CO2, som de selskaber vi
investere i, udleder. Reduktionerne kan opnås ved, at selskaberne reducerer deres udledninger, men også ved at vi løbende tilpasser vores investeringer, således
at vi i højere grad vælger at investere i selskaber, der har lavere CO2 udledning.

Vores realkreditmålsætninger dækker samlet omkring 50% af vores investeringer i realkreditobligationer.
For realkreditobligationer i investeringsforeningsafdelinger administreret i koncernen er CO2e-aftrykket reduceret med 51% siden 2019, og delmålet om 40%
reduktion i 2030 er således allerede opfyldt. Udviklingen i CO2e-aftrykket har været flad til svagt faldende for de forskellige realkreditinstitutter, hvilket afspejles
i en tilsvarende svagt faldende CO2e-udledning i de forvaltede afdelinger. Delmålet fastholdes for nuværende.
Den resterende AUM på ca. 20% er investeret i statsobligationer, virksomhedsobligationer samt alternativer. Der er ikke fastsat reduktionsmål på mellemlangt
sigte for denne del af AUM, da vi vurderer, at data og metode ikke er tilstrækkeligt udviklet til at kunne udarbejde en retvisende beregning og dermed levere en
valid målsætning. Vi inkluderer de resterende aktivtyper i takt med, at data og metoder tillader det.
Inden 2027 forventer vi at fastlægge 1,5 grads mål for virksomhedsobligationer.
For så vidt angår statsobligationer, er der ikke fundet en gængs metode, som ikke favoriserer stater, som outsourcer deres CO2-udledning til Emerging Market
lande.
Alternative investeringer er ikke dækket af PCAF og det er derfor p.t. ikke vurderet muligt at opsætte en metode.
Jyske Bank har tilsluttet sig Net Zero Asset Managers initiative (NZAM), hvor indmeldte mål mindst hvert femte år evalueres og tilmeldt AUM opjusteres, indtil
100% dækning er opnået. Det er generelt en del af NZAM forpligtelsen at understøtte realiseringen af Parisaftalens målsætninger. Den mere specifikke forpligtelse
overfor NZAM er formuleret således:
“Specifically, my organisation commits to:
a. Work in partnership with asset owner clients on decarbonisation goals, consistent with an ambition to reach net zero emissions by 2050 or sooner across all assets
under management (‘AUM’)
b. Set an interim target for the proportion of assets to be managed in line with the attainment of net zero emissions by 2050 or sooner
c. Review our interim target at least every five years, with a view to ratcheting up the proportion of AUM covered until 100% of assets are included”.
Ovenstående uddrag fra Net Zero Asset Managers Commitment beskriver, hvordan Jyske Bank forventer at fastsætte reduktionsmål for de resterende 30% af
bankens AUM – dog således at mål for virksomhedsobligationer forventes fastlagt inden 2027.
Opfølgning på vores reduktionsmål behandles to gange årligt i FABI med orientering til Koncernens Bæredygtighedsudvalg. Der rapporteres årligt på
målsætningerne i forbindelse med Jyske Banks årsrapportering. Opfølgningen benytter nøgletallet CO2-aftryk, som opgøres efter formlerne i Disclosure
14

Beskrivelse af de vigtigste negative indvirkninger på bæredygtighedsfaktorer
BIAM har udstedt en Politik for ansvarlige investeringer, hvor porteføljeforvalterne skal tage højde for de væsentligste negative
bæredygtighedsvirkninger (PAI-indikatorer) i deres investeringsbeslutninger. Hvilke PAI-indikatorer, der er identificeret og prioriteret i de enkelte
afdelinger, afhænger blandt andet af datakvalitet, tilgængelighed og relevans.
Alle afdelinger skal dog som minimum anvende oplysninger om udstedernes omsætning inden for udvalgte sektorer, ESG-rating samt foretage
normbaseret screening. Oplysninger om udstedernes drivhusgasemissioner er også identificeret som en vigtig PAI-indikator, og vil anvendes på
afdelinger med tilstrækkelig datakvalitet og -tilgængelighed.

matched_patterns: companies active in the fossil fuel sector
raw_indicator_text:
Principal Adverse Impact Statement 30 June 2025
These core areas of interest are linked to PAI indicators: 1.1 (GHG emissions),1.2 (Carbon footprint),1.3 (GHG intensity of investee companies),1.4 (Exposure to
companies active in the fossil fuel sector),1.5 (Share of non-renewable energy consumption and production),1.6 (Energy consumption intensity per high impact climate
sector),1.7 (Activities negatively affecting biodiversity-sensitive areas), 1.8 (Emissions to water),1.15 (GHG intensity) and 2.8 (Exposure to areas of high water stress).
These four core areas of interest have been identified as such due to the severe long-term consequences, that adverse impact may have on these matters and the
systemic risk they pose. We have developed certain ESG positions around these four core areas to describe the requirements that we have on investee companies in
relation hereto. The PAI indicators that we consider are aligned with our ESG positions and are subject to data quality and availability. Our ESG positions are further
described in our Responsible Investment Policy.
Considering principal adverse impact of investment decisions, i.e. the environmental and social impact of our activities, and sustainability risk, i.e. material negative impact
of ESG issues on the value of investments, captures the full scope of the double materiality concept. Information on how we integrate sustainability risk in the investment
decision-making process is available here.
Identification and assessment of PAI
Principal adverse impact assessment is integrated in our active ownership and engagement policies and processes and can be summarised as set out below.
As a starting point, we apply norm-based screening filters to identify companies that are allegedly involved in breaches of international law and norms on environmental
protection, human rights, labour standards and anti-corruption. These filters identify impact relating to some of the PAI indicators. To identify impact of our investment
decisions across all the mandatory and the additional PAI indicators that we have chosen to consider, we have developed a proprietary PAI monitoring system (PAI
engine).
The PAI engine is based on data acquired from third-party providers and investee companies. By ranking the performance of companies across each indicator, we aim to
identify each company's negative impact on climate and social issues as defined by the PAI metrics, both in absolute and relative terms (e.g. compared to peers). We
measure the overall exposure on entity level as well as the impact on product level, subject to data availability.
Companies identified by the PAI engine as outliers on specific indicators or that exhibit high adverse impact across several indicators, are analysed further by our
Responsible Investment team and a recommendation for action is made to our Responsible Investment Committee (RIC). RIC is presented with an update on the identified
company on a quarterly basis and may, based on this, decide on the appropriate action to be taken in each case.
14
14 Descriptions of actions taken under our active ownership and engagement polices and processes are referring to actions taken on behalf of the funds managed by us.

(e.g. engagement or exclusion).
At the end of 2024, NAM had

weapons. Two of these issuers
were excluded during 2024.

matched_patterns: companies active in the fossil fuel sector
raw_indicator_text:
Principal Adverse Impact Statement 30 June 2025
• Biodiversity
These core areas of interest are linked to PAI indicators: 1.1 (GHG emissions),1.2 (Carbon footprint),1.3 (GHG intensity of investee companies),1.4 (Exposure to
companies active in the fossil fuel sector),1.5 (Share of non-renewable energy consumption and production),1.6 (Energy consumption intensity per high impact climate
sector),1.7 (Activities negatively affecting biodiversity-sensitive areas), 1.8 (Emissions to water),1.15 (GHG intensity) and 2.8 (Exposure to areas of high water stress).
These four core areas of interest have been identified as such due to the severe long-term consequences, that adverse impact may have on these matters and the
systemic risk they pose. We have developed certain ESG positions around these four core areas to describe the requirements that we have on investee companies in
relation hereto. The PAI indicators that we consider are aligned with our ESG positions and are subject to data quality and availability. Our ESG positions are further
described in our Responsible Investment Policy.
Considering principal adverse impact of investment decisions, i.e. the environmental and social impact of our activities, and sustainability risk, i.e. material negative impact
of ESG issues on the value of investments, captures the full scope of the double materiality concept. Information on how we integrate sustainability risk in the investment
decision-making process is available here.
Identification and assessment of PAI
Principal adverse impact assessment is integrated in our active ownership and engagement policies and processes and can be summarised as set out below.
As a starting point, we apply norm-based screening filters to identify companies that are allegedly involved in breaches of international law and norms on environmental
protection, human rights, labour standards and anti-corruption. These filters identify impact relating to some of the PAI indicators. To identify impact of our investment
decisions across all the mandatory and the additional PAI indicators that we have chosen to consider, we have developed a proprietary PAI monitoring system (PAI
engine).
The PAI engine is based on data acquired from third-party providers and investee companies. By ranking the performance of companies across each indicator, we aim to
identify each company's negative impact on climate and social issues as defined by the PAI metrics, both in absolute and relative terms (e.g. compared to peers). We
measure the overall exposure on entity level as well as the impact on product level, subject to data availability.
